There are different measures we can use with fMRI, but the most classic one is to use the BOLD response, which stands for blood oxygen, B-O-L, oxygen level dependent response.
Basically, again, with fMRI, we don't measure neural activity directly.
We measure the delivery of oxygen because when neurons fire, there's feedback loops, probably driven by the astrocytes that say, hey, my neurons are firing here.
I'm going to tap into the blood supply and extract extra oxygen because I know I've already burned up a lot of the oxygen.

Brain cells use up a lot of oxygen, and so what happens is initially you deplete the oxygen in the active area, but that happens very quickly, and what really happens is that the brain compensates, overcompensates, and delivers more oxygen to an area that's been recently active, and the fMRI machine is set up, or the way people set it up, is that they're going to measure the magnetic properties that are different in oxygenated versus deoxygenated blood, and so in the end, we end up with a metabolic measure.

By metabolic, I mean it's really a measure of oxygen, but we use it as a proxy of neural activity, so if we're doing an fMRI experiment, that would be the basic one we'd be doing, so we're probably going to be measuring the bold, so we're going to put that, and usually we might even put it's going to be the change in the bold signal because, of course, oxygen is always being used, and as I mentioned earlier, you know, when you use a particular part of the brain, it might increase its level of oxygen by maybe one or two percent.
